pattern: Two nested modular exponentiations reduce to two independent fast-power computations per entry, so each index can be checked on its own in logarithmic time rather than by ever forming the true (astronomically large) power.
hints: You never need the real value of a number raised to a huge exponent -- only its remainder after a modulus -- so reach for repeated squaring instead of literal exponentiation.
Compute the inner remainder first (the base raised to its exponent, reduced modulo ten), then feed that small result into a second repeated-squaring step with the outer exponent and modulus.
Check each entry independently: after both modular exponentiations for an entry, compare the final remainder to the target and collect the entry's own position whenever it matches, in any order.

edge_inputs.description: three entries where two of them match the target
single entry that does not match the target
modulus of one always reduces to zero, matching a target of zero
every value at its maximum allowed magnitude
target equals the modulus itself, which a remainder can never reach
base of one collapses both exponentiations to one, so the match depends only on the modulus
several entries share the same inputs, so either all or none should be selected
large outer exponent exercising the repeated-squaring loop many times

stress.description: many entries with random values across the full allowed range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    variables = []
    for _ in range(n):
        a = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        b = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        c = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        m = rng.randint(1, 1000)
        variables.append([a, b, c, m])
    target = rng.randint(0, 1000)
    return [variables, target]
stress.note: random entries and target exercise both modular exponentiation steps at scale
